teamObjective.explanation: With only two cards left while opponents hold three each, the priority is to expend both cards as quickly as possible; leading the stronger card first maximizes the chance of winning the trick and retaining the lead for the final card.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner's exact cards are unknown; no public history beyond the new-game event provides evidence about their holdings.
evidence: No cards have been played or exposed.
No bids or communication have occurred.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents likely hold cards that can beat a low spade 2, but no specific cards are known.
evidence: Opponents each hold three unknown cards.
Rank 2 is the weakest natural card, so any non-2 non-trump card of the same or other suit likely beats it.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the Ace of spades is the strongest available move. If it wins the trick, I keep the lead and can play the 2 next to exit my hand. If it loses, the outcome is similar to leading the 2, but the Ace has a higher chance of winning.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 2 is the weakest natural card and will almost certainly be beaten by an opponent, surrendering the lead and giving opponents a chance to reduce their hands before I can play my Ace.

risks: An opponent may hold a trump or higher non-trump card that beats the Ace, causing me to lose the lead.
Even if the Ace wins, an opponent may still win the next trick before I can play my final card.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the Ace first is the best available mitigation because it maximizes the chance of retaining control and playing the final card on the following trick.

notes: No hidden cards are asserted as facts; the choice follows standard endgame race logic of leading high to retain the lead.
